title: List all divisors of N sorted
description: Given N, return all divisors in ascending order

examples.explanation: N=7. Initialize divisors=[]. Iterate i from 1 to 7: i=1, 7%1=0, append 1 → divisors=[1]. i=2, 7%2=1 ≠0, skip. i=3, 7%3=1 ≠0, skip. i=4, 7%4=3 ≠0, skip. i=5, 7%5=2 ≠0, skip. i=6, 7%6=1 ≠0, skip. i=7, 7%7=0, append 7 → [1,7]. Return [1,7].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from 1 to N, check if each number divides N evenly, and collect divisors. This is straightforward but inefficient for large N.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(d) where d is the number of divisors

ladders.title: Full Problem — List All Divisors of N in Ascending Order Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Given an integer N, return all its divisors sorted in ascending order using a brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem where we implement the brute force O(N) solution. The intuition is straightforward: every divisor d of N must satisfy N % d == 0, meaning d divides N without remainder. Since divisors come in pairs (d and N/d), we can find all divisors by checking every integer from 1 to N. However, this approach is inefficient for large N because it requires N iterations. The algorithm initializes an empty list to store divisors. It iterates i from 1 to N inclusive. For each i, if N % i == 0, i is a divisor and is appended to the list. After the loop, the list is sorted (though it will already be in ascending order due to iteration from 1 to N) and returned. Time complexity is O(N) due to the loop, and space complexity is O(d) where d is the number of divisors, for storing the list. This connects to L1 as it uses the key subroutine of checking divisibility for each number. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like N=0 or negative N (though typically N is positive), off-by-one errors in the loop range, or forgetting to sort if the order isn't guaranteed. Real code pattern: def list_divisors(N): divisors = []; for i in range(1, N+1): if N % i == 0: divisors.append(i); return divisors.

examples.trace: N=7. Initialize divisors=[]. Iterate i from 1 to 7: i=1, 7%1=0, append 1 → divisors=[1]. i=2, 7%2=1 ≠0, skip. i=3, 7%3=1 ≠0, skip. i=4, 7%4=3 ≠0, skip. i=5, 7%5=2 ≠0, skip. i=6, 7%6=1 ≠0, skip. i=7, 7%7=0, append 7 → [1,7]. Return [1,7].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Divisibility for a Single Number
ladders.desc: Given N and a candidate divisor i, determine if i divides N evenly.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function in the brute force approach. The intuition is based on the definition of divisibility: i is a divisor of N if and only if the remainder when N is divided by i is zero. This is a fundamental arithmetic operation that underpins the entire algorithm. The algorithm takes inputs N and i, computes N % i (modulo operation), and returns True if the result is 0, otherwise False. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves a constant-time modulo operation, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly in the loop from 1 to N to collect all divisors. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division, not handling i=0 (which would cause division by zero, but in this context i starts from 1), or incorrect comparison (e.g., checking N/i is integer instead of N%i==0). Real code pattern: def is_divisor(N, i): return N % i == 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through All Possible Divisors
ladders.desc: Loop from 1 to N and apply divisibility check to each number.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of the brute force approach is that to find all divisors, we must examine every integer in the range [1, N]. This works because any divisor of N must lie within this range (since divisors cannot exceed N in value). The technique involves a simple for-loop that systematically tests each candidate. The algorithm initializes an empty list, then for i from 1 to N inclusive, if i divides N (using the modulo check), append i to the list. After the loop, the list contains all divisors in ascending order due to the increasing i. Time complexity is O(N) as the loop runs N times, with O(1) work per iteration. Space complexity is O(d) for the list. This connects to L1 by using the divisibility check as the condition inside the loop. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using range(1, N) which excludes N), inefficiency by not stopping at sqrt(N) (but that's a different approach), or incorrectly modifying the loop variable. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, N+1): if N % i == 0: divisors.append(i).

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when N is divided by i.
ladders.explanation: An intermediate concept needed for the divisibility check is the modulo operation, which finds the remainder of division. The intuition is that if N divided by i leaves no remainder, then i is a divisor. Mathematically, N % i = N - i * floor(N/i). This operation is crucial because it directly tests divisibility. The algorithm takes N and i, performs N % i, and returns the integer remainder. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L2 as it is used in the condition N % i == 0 within the loop. Common mistakes include confusing % with / (division), not understanding that modulo with negative numbers can be tricky (but here N and i are positive), or assuming it works only for integers. Real code pattern: remainder = N % i.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Comparison
ladders.desc: Perform division on integers and compare values.
ladders.explanation: A fundamental operation used in the modulo check is integer division and comparison. The intuition is that to check if i divides N, we need to see if N/i is an integer, which is equivalent to N % i == 0. This involves dividing two integers and checking the result. The algorithm computes quotient = N // i (integer division) and then checks if N == quotient * i. Alternatively, directly use modulo. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as it underpins the modulo operation. Common mistakes include using floating-point division (/) which can lead to precision errors, not handling division by zero, or incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: if N == (N // i) * i: return True else False.

examples.trace: Compute quotient = 12 // 4 = 3. Check if 12 == 3 * 4 = 12. Yes, so return True.
Compute quotient = 12 // 5 = 2. Check if 12 == 2 * 5 = 10. No, so return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integers and Division
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic with integers: what are divisors and how division works.
ladders.explanation: The simplest prerequisite concept is understanding integers and division. The intuition is that an integer a is a divisor of integer b if there exists an integer c such that b = a * c. This is the foundational definition from number theory. For example, 3 is a divisor of 12 because 12 = 3 * 4. This concept is essential because the entire problem revolves around identifying such numbers. The algorithm here is conceptual: given b and a, check if b divided by a results in an integer with no remainder. Time and space complexity are not applicable as this is a conceptual understanding. This connects to L4 as it explains why we use integer division and comparison. Common mistakes include confusing divisors with multiples, not recognizing that 1 and the number itself are always divisors, or misunderstanding negative numbers (though typically we consider positive divisors). Real code pattern: Conceptual, no code—just the mathematical definition.

examples.trace: Check if there exists an integer c such that 8 = 2 * c. Solve: c = 8 / 2 = 4, which is an integer. So yes, 2 is a divisor.

examples.output: No, 3 is not a divisor of 8
examples.trace: Check if there exists an integer c such that 8 = 3 * c. Solve: c = 8 / 3 ≈ 2.666, which is not an integer. So no, 3 is not a divisor.

testCases.expected: No, 3 is not a divisor of 10
No, 4 is not a divisor of 14
No, 8 is not a divisor of 18
No, 10 is not a divisor of 25

approaches.name: Square Root Optimization O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Iterate up to sqrt(N), find divisors in pairs, and sort the result. This reduces iterations significantly by using the fact that divisors come in pairs.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(d) where d is the number of divisors

ladders.title: Full Problem — List All Divisors of N Sorted Using Square Root Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all divisors of N in ascending order using O(√n) iteration.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem solution using the square root optimization approach. The intuition is that divisors always come in pairs (i, n/i), so we only need to iterate up to √n to find all divisors. For each i from 1 to √n, if i divides n, we add both i and n/i to our collection. Since we're iterating in increasing order, the smaller divisor (i) will be added in order, but the larger divisor (n/i) might not be in order. We collect all divisors, then sort them at the end. The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty list 'divisors'. 2. For i from 1 to floor(√n): a. If n % i == 0: i. Add i to divisors. ii. If i != n/i, add n/i to divisors. 3. Sort divisors. 4. Return divisors. Time complexity is O(√n + d log d) where d is number of divisors (sorting cost). Space is O(d) to store divisors. This connects to L1 by using the divisor pair collection as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to check i != n/i when adding the pair (would add duplicate for perfect squares), not handling n=0 or n=1 correctly, or inefficiently iterating up to n instead of √n. Real code pattern: def get_divisors(n): divisors = []; for i in range(1, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % i == 0: divisors.append(i); if i != n//i: divisors.append(n//i); divisors.sort(); return divisors.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Collect Divisor Pairs Up to Square Root
ladders.desc: Find all divisor pairs (i, n/i) by iterating up to √n.
ladders.explanation: This is the core subroutine that makes the optimization work. The intuition is mathematical: if i divides n, then n/i also divides n, forming a pair. By only checking up to √n, we guarantee we'll find each pair exactly once because when i ≤ √n, then n/i ≥ √n. The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty list 'pairs'. 2. For i from 1 to floor(√n): a. If n % i == 0: i. Add i to pairs. ii. If i != n/i, add n/i to pairs. 3. Return pairs (unsorted). Time is O(√n), space O(d). This feeds into L0 by providing the divisor collection that needs sorting. Common mistakes: not using floor(√n) correctly (off-by-one), adding duplicate for perfect squares, or misunderstanding the pair relationship. Real code pattern: def collect_pairs(n): pairs = []; limit = int(n**0.5); for i in range(1, limit+1): if n % i == 0: pairs.append(i); if i != n//i: pairs.append(n//i); return pairs.

examples.trace: n=9, √9=3, limit=3: i=1: 9%1=0, add 1, add 9 → [1,9]; i=2: 9%2=1 (skip); i=3: 9%3=0, add 3, check 3!=9/3=3? false, so don't add duplicate → [1,9,3]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Pair Property and Square Root Bound
ladders.desc: Understand that divisors come in pairs and √n is the optimal iteration limit.
ladders.explanation: This is the mathematical insight behind the optimization. Intuition: For any divisor i of n, there exists a complementary divisor j = n/i. These form pairs (i, j). When i ≤ j, then i ≤ √n and j ≥ √n. Proof: If i ≤ j, then i² ≤ i*j = n, so i ≤ √n. Therefore, by iterating i from 1 to √n, we'll find one element from each pair. The algorithm concept: 1. Recognize divisor pair property. 2. Determine iteration limit as floor(√n). 3. For each i ≤ √n that divides n, capture both i and n/i. Time analysis: Without this insight, we'd iterate O(n) times; with it, O(√n). This feeds into L1 by providing the logic for the collection loop. Common mistakes: not understanding why √n is the boundary, or thinking pairs are always distinct (they're identical when n is a perfect square). Real code pattern: The insight informs the loop design: limit = int(math.sqrt(n)); for i in range(1, limit+1).

examples.trace: Divisors of 20: 1,2,4,5,10,20. Pairs: 1*20=20, 2*10=20, 4*5=20. √20≈4.47, so i ≤4: i=1 gives pair (1,20), i=2 gives (2,10), i=3 not divisor, i=4 gives (4,5). All pairs captured.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check Divisibility and Compute Complementary Divisor
ladders.desc: Determine if a number divides another and find the paired divisor.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two operations: checking if a divides b (b % a == 0), and if so, computing the complementary divisor b/a. Intuition: Division is the inverse of multiplication; if a is a divisor, then b/a is the integer that multiplies with a to give b. The algorithm: For given a and b: 1. Check if b % a == 0 (remainder 0). 2. If true, compute complementary = b // a (integer division). Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the operations needed to find and record divisor pairs. Common mistakes: using floating-point division instead of integer division, or not handling division by zero. Real code pattern: if n % i == 0: complement = n // i.

examples.trace: Check 12 % 3 = 0, so 3 divides 12. Compute complement = 12 // 3 = 4. Pair is (3,4).
Check 12 % 5 = 2 (not 0), so 5 does not divide 12. No complement.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo
ladders.desc: Perform integer division (//) and modulo (%) operations.
ladders.explanation: These are fundamental arithmetic operations. Integer division a // b gives the quotient (floor of a/b), and modulo a % b gives the remainder. Intuition: For integers a and b>0, a = (a // b) * b + (a % b), with 0 ≤ a % b < b. Checking a % b == 0 tests if b divides a evenly. The algorithm: Given a and b: 1. Compute quotient = a // b. 2. Compute remainder = a % b. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the tools for divisibility checks. Common mistakes: confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), or not understanding modulo with negative numbers (but here we deal with positive n). Real code pattern: quotient = n // i; remainder = n % i.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Divisor?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition of a divisor of an integer.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: for integers a and b, a is a divisor of b if there exists an integer k such that b = a * k. In other words, a divides b evenly with no remainder. Key properties: 1 is always a divisor of any integer. The number itself is always a divisor. Divisors are always positive in this context (though negative divisors exist, we focus on positives). Intuition: Divisors are the numbers you can multiply together to get the original number. The algorithm concept: To test if a is a divisor of b, check if b % a == 0. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L4 by defining what we're checking with modulo. Common mistakes: thinking divisors must be less than the number (they can be equal), or confusing with multiples. Real code pattern: The concept underlies the condition: if n % i == 0, then i is a divisor.
